author: @Gwydion67
text:
Awsome!👌
Definitely something, I will add to my exercise bike. Still working in home office most of the time, sitting 9h+ in front of my Laptop would be the best occasion at all. I miss the physical activities, walk around my apartment several thousand steps in parallel, but missing pedalling my bicycle nevertheless. The whole office equipment takes between 20 and 60 Watts, let's say 40 on average.
Ok, a handcrank won't be feasible for that application, but by sitting and pedalling on the bike I coukd work nearly normally.
Adding a DC-DC converter (charger) and small battery (~1-2 kWh), this would guarantee for a self-sufficient home office (including lights in the evening and keeping some USB devices charged).

Well, so much for my own idea of some appliance. I'm curious about your own ones and will jump right into your next video... CU there! 👋😃

author: @psylentrage
text: Shot, Luke! Nice little machine. I really do believe alternators are where it's at. Been saying it for a while. The main head scratch is just to get it to turn at about 2000rpm while being kept cool. And youcan get them everywhere and fairly easy to maintain, and the car model is of no real consequence. On Victron Energy's channel, they have a way of charging lithium batteries with an alternator...

author: @peterclark6290
text:
Now build a room full of them. Put a sign outside that says 'Job Centre'. Pay any users for their specific output; and kill two stones with one bird. Work equates to value. Nothing is 'free'. 
Add a small kitchen selling good food and the results keep compounding: good tucker = more energy = elevated fitness = more work capacity = more good food and bonus cash.
OK, I have re-invented the workhouse (Me very very bad). Now for some real world examples. How many turns would it take to generate enough power to run a microwave at 60% for seven minutes? The energy required to cook a medium-sized, cleaned, not-skinned, whole potato to mash ready. Or, how many cranks would it take to do the same work in real time?

author: @angelusmendez5084
text: Awesome! 👏

author: @paulwright5440
text: It be good to see a diagram of the wiring,I can't see where you put power in an out

author: @deslomeslager
text: Try a lamp from a car, the front one. It should be 12 Volt 60 Watt. I am sure it will light up as bright as it should, and you will feel how much power it takes to generate those 60 Watts. PS: I could make a movie and show you how I do it, with almost none conversion ! ! ! (it is just an existing e-bike) I think, if you wan to show people a real easy way, use an e-bike. It is still usable as a bike, and when at home you can use it to generate power. I can even warm up water for a cup of coffee (using my legs of course).

author: @401ksolar
text: Luke, I have been experimenting with such handpowered devices since the 1970s, it's been my observation that either gears or chains and sprockets can give you much less friction

replies.author: @Flashahol
replies.text: Because it inherently has a lot less tension than a belt I'm guessing??

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: That is awesome! I was thinking about chain and gears, because you're right they do have a bit less friction. I might have to rethink this project because that would be awesome! Thanks mate!😁😁

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: @Flashahol try a bike with a belt then get back with us with your results LOL
